20580.headline: Took the Hubble Deep Field (1995)
20580.detail: A 10-day exposure of a seemingly empty patch of sky that revealed roughly 3,000 previously unseen galaxies, reshaping how astronomers estimate the number of galaxies in the universe.

20580.headline: Helped pin down the age of the universe
20580.detail: Hubble's Key Project measured the universe's expansion rate (the Hubble Constant) precisely enough to put the universe's age at roughly 13.7-13.8 billion years.

25867.headline: Imaged the neutron star at the heart of Cassiopeia A
25867.detail: Chandra's X-ray images of the Cassiopeia A supernova remnant revealed the compact neutron star left behind by the explosion, along with the expanding debris shockwave.

25989.headline: One of ESA's most productive X-ray observatories
25989.detail: Launched in 1999, XMM-Newton carries the largest X-ray mirrors ever built and has been used in thousands of scientific papers on black holes, neutron stars, and galaxy clusters.

33053.headline: Discovered the 'Fermi Bubbles' (2010)
33053.detail: Fermi's gamma-ray survey of the sky revealed two enormous, previously-unknown gamma-ray-emitting structures extending above and below the Milky Way's center.

36395.headline: Continuous whole-Sun imaging since 2010
36395.detail: The Solar Dynamics Observatory has captured the Sun in multiple wavelengths nearly continuously since launch, building one of the largest public archives of solar imagery ever produced.

49954.headline: First mission dedicated to X-ray polarization (2021)
49954.detail: IXPE is the first satellite built specifically to measure the polarization of X-rays from black holes, neutron stars, and supernova remnants -- about 100x more sensitive to it than any earlier mission.

38358.headline: First focusing high-energy X-ray telescope in orbit
38358.detail: NuSTAR uses precisely aligned nested mirrors to focus high-energy X-rays, a first for a space telescope, letting it image black holes and supernova remnants far more sharply than earlier X-ray missions.

28485.headline: First-of-its-kind autonomous rapid-slewing observatory
28485.detail: Launched Nov 20, 2004, Swift can detect a gamma-ray burst and autonomously slew to point its telescopes at it within seconds, and now finds roughly 100 gamma-ray bursts a year.

29479.headline: High-resolution studies of the Sun's magnetic field
29479.detail: Launched Sept 23, 2006 by JAXA as successor to the Yohkoh mission, Hinode simultaneously observes the Sun's photosphere through corona, revealing how magnetic energy drives solar explosions.

25544.headline: Continuously crewed since November 2, 2000
25544.detail: The ISS has hosted an unbroken chain of crews for over two decades -- over 270 people from more than 20 countries have visited it.

25544.headline: Largest human-made structure ever put into space
25544.detail: At 109m by 73m with its solar arrays extended, the ISS is roughly the size of a full football field -- the largest single structure humans have ever assembled in orbit.

36086.headline: First major Russian ISS addition since 2001
36086.detail: The Poisk module launched Nov 10, 2009 and docked to Zvezda's zenith port -- Russia's first new ISS module in eight years.

49044.headline: Russia's largest spacecraft launch since 2000
49044.detail: The 20-ton Nauka module launched July 21, 2021 -- Russia's largest single spacecraft sent to the ISS since the original Zvezda service module two decades earlier.

48274.headline: Core module of China's first long-term space station
48274.detail: Tianhe launched April 29, 2021 as the first and largest module of Tiangong, China's first space station meant for permanent crewed habitation.

53239.headline: Tiangong's first science lab module
53239.detail: Wentian launched July 24, 2022, adding a dedicated science airlock and experiment racks to China's Tiangong space station.

54216.headline: Completed the Tiangong space station
54216.detail: Mengtian launched Oct 31, 2022 -- the third and final module, completing Tiangong's basic three-module T-shaped configuration.

39084.headline: Part of the longest continuous space-based record of Earth's land surface
39084.detail: Landsat 8 continues a program running since 1972 -- the longest continuous satellite record of Earth's land surface, used to track everything from deforestation to urban growth.

25994.headline: Flagship of NASA's Earth Observing System
25994.detail: Launched Dec 18, 1999, Terra was the first of the EOS series (followed by Aqua and Aura) and its instruments have produced one of the longest continuous climate data records ever collected by satellite.

27424.headline: Second EOS flagship, in orbit over 20 years
27424.detail: Launched May 2002 carrying six instruments (AIRS, AMSU-A, CERES, MODIS, AMSR-E, HSB), Aqua has run for more than double its planned lifetime, tracking water in Earth's atmosphere, oceans, and ice.

37849.headline: Bridged NASA's EOS satellites to the next-generation JPSS series
37849.detail: Launched Oct 28, 2011, Suomi NPP's VIIRS instrument produced NASA's famous high-resolution 'Black Marble' night-lights image of Earth in 2012.

43013.headline: First satellite of NOAA's JPSS series
43013.detail: Launched Nov 18, 2017 as JPSS-1, NOAA-20 provides roughly an order of magnitude more data than the earlier EOS satellites it helps succeed.

33591.headline: Last of NOAA's original POES weather-satellite series
33591.detail: Launched Feb 6, 2009, NOAA-19 was the fifth and final satellite in NOAA's Polar Operational Environmental Satellite series, which was retired after 47 years of continuous service in August 2025.

41866.headline: First of NOAA's GOES-R series
41866.detail: GOES-16 carries the first operational Geostationary Lightning Mapper and imagery roughly 4x sharper and 5x faster than the GOES satellites it replaced.

51850.headline: Operational GOES West satellite
51850.detail: Launched as GOES-T in March 2022, it became GOES-18 and took over as the operational GOES West satellite on Jan 4, 2023, watching the Pacific and US West Coast.

40732.headline: Last of the Meteosat Second Generation satellites
40732.detail: Launched July 15, 2015 as MSG-4, it was renamed Meteosat-11 after commissioning and held in orbital storage until needed -- the final satellite of Europe's highly successful MSG series.

38771.headline: Real-time global ocean wind measurements
38771.detail: Part of EUMETSAT's Metop series, Metop-B's ASCAT instrument has provided continuous real ocean-surface wind speed/direction data used in operational weather forecasting worldwide.

41335.headline: First of the Copernicus Sentinel-3 ocean-monitoring satellites
41335.detail: Launched Feb 16, 2016, Sentinel-3A was the first of four planned Sentinel-3 satellites dedicated to measuring ocean color, sea/land surface temperature, and sea-level height for Europe's climate program.

32382.headline: First fully commercial multi-polarization SAR satellite
32382.detail: Launched Dec 2007, Canada's RADARSAT-2 was the first fully commercial radar satellite to offer multi-polarization imaging, resolving detail down to 3 meters.

38012.headline: Sub-meter commercial Earth imagery
38012.detail: Launched Dec 17, 2011, Pleiades 1A captures color imagery at 70cm resolution (resampled to 50cm) -- sharp enough to make out individual vehicles from orbit.

39574.headline: First spaceborne dual-frequency precipitation radar
39574.detail: GPM Core Observatory's Ku/Ka-band Dual-frequency Precipitation Radar was the first of its kind in orbit, resolving the 3D structure of rain and snow rather than just a 2D estimate.

35951.headline: Part of the longest-running production satellite program ever
35951.detail: DMSP has flown continuously since the 1960s and provided the first satellite imagery ever used to support tactical military operations, during the Vietnam War.

39089.headline: World's first satellite dedicated to hunting asteroids
39089.detail: Launched in 2013, NEOSSat was the first space telescope built specifically to detect and track asteroids, comets, and satellites/debris from orbit.

26407.headline: Part of GPS, the world's first global satellite navigation system
26407.detail: This Block IIR satellite is one of the workhorses that kept GPS running as the world's original and most widely used GNSS constellation.

36585.headline: Part of GPS's modernized Block IIF generation
36585.detail: Block IIF satellites like this one added the L5 civil safety-of-life signal, improving GPS accuracy and reliability for the world's original GNSS.

40105.headline: Part of GPS's modernized Block IIF generation
40105.detail: Block IIF satellites like this one added the L5 civil safety-of-life signal, improving GPS accuracy and reliability for the world's original GNSS.

37846.headline: Part of Galileo, Europe's first independent satellite navigation system
37846.detail: As one of Galileo's first (In-Orbit Validation) satellites, GSAT0101 helped prove out the design of a constellation that reached its full 26-satellite build-out in July 2018.

40128.headline: Part of Galileo, Europe's first independent satellite navigation system
40128.detail: This satellite helped build out Galileo's constellation, which reached full operational capability with 26 satellites in July 2018.

36828.headline: Part of BeiDou's regional second generation
36828.detail: This BeiDou-2 IGSO satellite helped provide China and the Asia-Pacific region navigation coverage before BeiDou expanded into a full 35-satellite global constellation, completed in 2020.

43001.headline: Part of BeiDou's completed global constellation
43001.detail: This BeiDou-3 satellite is one of the 35 satellites that made BeiDou a fully global GNSS in 2020, using a unique hybrid MEO/GEO/IGSO design for extra Asia-Pacific accuracy.

32275.headline: Part of GLONASS, Russia's global satellite navigation system
32275.detail: This GLONASS-M satellite is one of the workhorses of Russia's independently-operated global GNSS constellation.

36111.headline: Part of GLONASS, Russia's global satellite navigation system
36111.detail: This GLONASS-M satellite is one of the workhorses of Russia's independently-operated global GNSS constellation.

46052.headline: Part of the largest satellite constellation ever launched
46052.detail: Starlink accounts for roughly 69% of all active satellites in Earth orbit as of 2026 -- more than every other satellite operator on Earth combined.

44714.headline: Part of the largest satellite constellation ever launched
44714.detail: Starlink accounts for roughly 69% of all active satellites in Earth orbit as of 2026 -- more than every other satellite operator on Earth combined.

44718.headline: Part of the largest satellite constellation ever launched
44718.detail: Starlink accounts for roughly 69% of all active satellites in Earth orbit as of 2026 -- more than every other satellite operator on Earth combined.

44057.headline: Part of the OneWeb global broadband constellation
44057.detail: OneWeb reached full global coverage in March 2023 after launching its 618th satellite -- this satellite is one of the fleet that made that possible.

45131.headline: Part of the OneWeb global broadband constellation
45131.detail: OneWeb reached full global coverage in March 2023 after launching its 618th satellite -- this satellite is one of the fleet that made that possible.

41917.headline: Part of the completed Iridium NEXT constellation
41917.detail: Iridium NEXT finished its $3 billion, 75-satellite upgrade in February 2019 and carries the real Aireon payload -- the first satellite-based global air-traffic tracking system.

41918.headline: Part of the completed Iridium NEXT constellation
41918.detail: Iridium NEXT finished its $3 billion, 75-satellite upgrade in February 2019 and carries the real Aireon payload -- the first satellite-based global air-traffic tracking system.

24278.headline: One of the longest-serving amateur radio satellites
24278.detail: Launched Aug 17, 1996 from Japan, FO-29 (JAS-2) still carries a working linear transponder for two-way voice/CW contacts nearly three decades later.

26931.headline: Built by US Naval Academy midshipmen
26931.detail: Launched Sept 30, 2001, PCSAT is an APRS digipeater built by student midshipmen, letting anyone with a handheld radio relay location/text messages through it worldwide.

35932.headline: The first Swiss satellite
35932.detail: Launched Sept 23, 2009, SwissCube was Switzerland's first satellite, built by university students to study nightglow in Earth's upper atmosphere.

35933.headline: First 1U CubeSat with three reaction wheels
35933.detail: Launched Sept 23, 2009 (the same rocket as SwissCube), TU Berlin's BEESAT-1 was the first single-unit CubeSat ever flown with a full three-axis reaction wheel attitude control system.

7530.headline: The 'satellite that refused to die'
7530.detail: Launched in 1974, AO-7 went silent after a battery failure in 1981 -- then, in 2002, one of its shorted battery cells failed open, letting it run directly off solar power and come back to life 21+ years later. It's the oldest still-operating amateur radio satellite.
